mechanism: Cortisol stimulates sebaceous glands and increases adrenal androgen production. Stress elevates cortisol 50-100% above baseline → sebum overproduction → acne flares. CRH (keratinocyte-derived) also directly stimulates sebocytes.

Cortisol → barrier compromise (reduced filaggrin, loricrin, ceramides) → allergen penetration → inflammation → itch-scratch cycle. Stress precedes 67% of eczema flares. Sleep deprivation elevates cortisol 37-45%.
Stress → sympathetic activation → IL-6, TNF-α release → keratinocyte hyperproliferation via Th17 pathway. 40-80% of patients report stress as trigger. MBSR reduces psoriasis severity.
Stress is #1 rosacea trigger. CRH stimulates mast cells → histamine and inflammatory mediator release → flushing and neurovascular reactivity. Chronic stress maintains vascular hyperresponsiveness.

Stress may trigger autoimmune attack on hair follicles via immune dysregulation. Chronic cortisol shifts immune balance and may reduce Treg function, allowing autoreactive T-cells to attack follicles.

Cortisol binds GR in fibroblasts → upregulates MMP-1 (2-3 fold), MMP-3, MMP-9 → degrades collagen types I and III. Simultaneously suppresses procollagen gene transcription → 30-40% reduced collagen synthesis. Chronic stress correlates with perceived skin aging.

Exogenous or endogenous cortisol excess (Cushing's, chronic topical/systemic steroid use) → dermal collagen and elastin degradation → striae rubrae formation. Striae are a hallmark of iatrogenic Cushing's.

Cortisol suppresses neutrophil/macrophage recruitment, inhibits fibroblast proliferation and collagen synthesis, reduces VEGF, and impairs angiogenesis. Caregivers of dementia patients (chronic stress) show 24-40% slower wound healing.

Estrogen withdrawal post-menopause → collagen loss 2-5% per year (first 5 years), skin thinning 1-2% per year, HA depletion, reduced elasticity. Estrogen increases procollagen I/III mRNA and TIMP-1; inhibits MMP-1 via AP-1 suppression.
Estrogen upregulates HA synthase 2 (HAS2), ceramide synthesis, and tight junction proteins (claudin-1, occludin). Deficiency → HA loss → reduced water retention, dryness, impaired barrier.
Loss of estrogen-driven collagen synthesis (types I and III via TGF-β1) and MMP-1 suppression → net collagen loss → dermal thinning. Skin thickness decreases 7-15% with estrogen withdrawal.
Estrogen supports inflammation phase and collagen synthesis during wound healing. Postmenopausal women show delayed healing due to reduced inflammatory response and collagen deposition.

Estrogen stimulates melanogenesis via MITF upregulation and tyrosinase activation. Progesterone may synergize. Hormonal contraceptives commonly trigger melasma. Pregnancy (high estrogen) is major trigger.
Melasma often improves post-menopause when estrogen declines, but this is relative to progesterone and androgen balance. HRT can worsen melasma.
Estrogen supports vascular endothelial health and dermal microcirculation. Deficiency contributes to telangiectasia, dull complexion, and reduced nutrient delivery to skin.

Progesterone binds AR in sebaceous glands (lower affinity than DHT). Luteal phase progesterone rise increases sebum 30-50% → premenstrual acne flares. Androgenic progestins (levonorgestrel, norethindrone) worsen acne.

Cyclical jawline/chin acne correlates with luteal phase progesterone rise. Relative estrogen deficiency (inadequate estrogen to offset progesterone/androgen effects) drives lesions. Post-pill acne follows progesterone withdrawal.

Progesterone may synergize with estrogen in stimulating melanogenesis. Some evidence that progesterone alone can trigger melasma. Combined oral contraceptives (estrogen + progestin) commonly trigger melasma.
Progesterone effects on skin thickness are complex and less studied than estrogen. Some evidence that progesterone may have mild anti-inflammatory effects via glucocorticoid receptor modulation.

DHT binds AR on sebocytes with 5× higher affinity than testosterone → lipogenic enzyme upregulation (FAS, ACC, SCD) → sebum overproduction. Also upregulates TLR2 → enhanced inflammatory response to C. acnes.
Androgens are primary driver of adult female jawline/chin acne. PCOS (70-80% have acne) is major underlying cause. DHT-driven sebum + follicular hyperkeratinization → deep inflammatory lesions.
citation: hormone-skin-connection.md: Section 1.4; clinical-guidelines.md: Acne hormonal therapy

mechanism: DHT binds AR in hair follicle dermal papilla cells → converts vellus to terminal hairs in androgen-sensitive areas (upper lip, chin, chest, abdomen, back, inner thighs). Requires elevated androgens AND genetic AR sensitivity.

DHT binds AR in scalp dermal papilla → shortens anagen phase → follicle miniaturization. Increases TGF-β1/β2 (catagen promotion) and reduces Wnt/β-catenin (pro-growth). IL-6 and TNF-α contribute to follicular inflammation.
Androgen-driven sebum provides substrate for Malassezia overgrowth. Sebaceous gland hyperplasia creates lipid-rich environment favoring Malassezia proliferation.
Hyperinsulinemia (often with androgen excess in PCOS) reduces SHBG → more free testosterone. Insulin and IGF-1 directly stimulate keratinocyte proliferation in flexural areas.
Hyperinsulinemia → elevated IGF-1 → mTORC1 activation → SREBP-1 upregulation → lipogenesis + keratinocyte hyperproliferation. Also reduces SHBG and IGFBP-3 → more free androgens and IGF-1.
Insulin and IGF-1 directly stimulate keratinocyte and fibroblast proliferation in flexural areas via IGF-1 receptor signaling → velvety hyperpigmented plaques.
Insulin reduces SHBG → more free testosterone → androgen receptor activation in hair follicles. Also stimulates ovarian androgen production in PCOS.
Insulin and IGF-1 stimulate fibroblast and keratinocyte proliferation in skin folds → acrochordons formation.
Hyperglycemia → non-enzymatic glycation → AGE formation on collagen → cross-linking → stiffness, reduced elasticity, yellowing. RAGE signaling activates NF-κB → IL-6, TNF-α, MMP-9.
Insulin resistance is common in PCOS (underlying cause of hormonal acne). Hyperinsulinemia amplifies ovarian androgen production and directly stimulates sebocytes via IGF-1/mTORC1.

Hypothyroidism reduces sebum production, impairs barrier lipid synthesis, and reduces epidermal turnover → severe dryness, scaling, cool pale skin. Myxedema causes non-pitting edema from mucopolysaccharide accumulation.
Both hypo- and hyperthyroidism cause diffuse non-scarring alopecia via altered follicle cycling. Hypothyroidism: brittle hair, dry scalp. Hyperthyroidism: fine, brittle hair, warm moist skin.
Hyperthyroidism can cause warm, moist skin but also fine hair and heat intolerance. Excessive sweating may paradoxically cause skin irritation.
Hypothyroidism impairs all phases of wound healing due to reduced metabolic rate, poor circulation, and impaired immune function.
DHEA is precursor to androgens. Excess DHEA-S (often from adrenal hyperplasia or PCOS) converts to testosterone and DHT → sebum overproduction.
Adrenal androgen excess (elevated DHEA-S) contributes to hirsutism, especially in non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia.
DHEA declines with age (adrenopause). Low DHEA correlates with skin atrophy and decreased sebum. Some evidence for DHEA supplementation improving skin thickness in elderly.
GH stimulates IGF-1 → supports collagen synthesis, cell proliferation, and dermal thickness. Deficiency causes thin skin; excess (acromegaly) causes skin thickening and oily skin.
GH/IGF-1 essential for cell proliferation and collagen synthesis during wound healing. Peak GH during deep sleep drives nocturnal repair.
Melatonin is potent antioxidant (scavenges hydroxyl radical, peroxynitrite). Peak at 2-4 AM neutralizes UV-induced free radicals. Sleep deprivation reduces melatonin → increased oxidative damage.
Melatonin has oncostatic properties and protects against UV-induced DNA damage. Reduced melatonin from poor sleep or blue light exposure may increase skin cancer risk.
Hyperprolactinemia can cause acne, hirsutism, and menstrual irregularities via effects on hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Dopamine agonists (if elevated) may improve skin.
Elevated prolactin postpartum suppresses ovulation and estrogen → relative androgen excess → postpartum acne and hair loss.
